coredata.dc:title: Cost-effective route to nanodiamonds from low-rank coal and their fluorescent & dielectric characteristics
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Raj, Ashlin M.
Manoj, Balachandran
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of nanodiamonds from abundant and inexpensive precursors has recently piqued the curiosity of researchers. It has the potential to significantly reduce the cost of nanodiamonds and open up a plethora of new applications. In this work, fluorescent nanodiamonds with smaller particle sizes with rich surface functionality are synthesized from low-grade coal lignite by employing a facile acidic oxidation and ultrasonication approach. The extracted nanodiamond particles are hydrophilic and display high excitation-dependent fluorescence in the aqueous medium. The excitation-dependent fluorescence can be ascribed to the collaboration and competition of the OH and COOH functional groups. The as-synthesized nanodiamonds also show good dielectric permittivity and ac conductivity over a wide frequency range at room temperature. The present research opens up the possibility of mass production of nanodiamonds on the industrial scale from a low-cost precursor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coal
Nanodiamonds
Fluorescence
Dielectric
Conductivity

We also acknowledge the CeNSE, Bangalore; M G University, Kottayam; and SAIF, CUSAT, Cochin, for the samples' characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of twinned agglomerated ND. Double yellow lines represent lattice planes, and the numbers indicate d-spacings in nm. Arrows are parallel to the common twinning plane. a) Accordion twin lonsdaleite, which exhibits folded, pleat-like lattice planes, and b) Twin with multiple folds. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Diffraction rings representing the (111), (220), and (311) planes of diamond.
HRTEM images showing the particle size distribution of disaggregated ND (50 nm resolution).
Particle size distribution of ND (measured using Fig. 3).
3D representation of the Atomic force microscopy image of ND with the particle size being marked.
XRD spectrum of the as-synthesized ND exhibiting the diamond plane.
Raman spectrum of the as-synthesized ND exhibiting nanodiamond peak, G peak and D peak.
FTIR spectrum of the as-synthesized ND.
High-resolution a) C 1s and b) O1s XPS spectra of as-synthesized nanodiamond.
The nanodiamond solution at two different concentrations indicating the hydrophilic nature.
UV Visible absorption spectrum of nanodiamond.
a) Fluorescence emission spectrum of ND for varying excitation wavelength and b) Representation of fluorescence origin.

Dependence of (a) Stokes shift (red line shows the linear fitting), (b) Fluorescence peak emission wavelength (red line shows the linear fitting), and (c) Intensity shift with the excitation wavelength. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
pH dependence of fluorescence emission spectrum.

(a–c). Dielectric properties and conductivity (a). The real part of the complex permittivity of the ND (b) imaginary part of the complex permittivity of the ND (c) AC conductivity of the ND vs. frequency.
The real part of the impedance of ND vs. frequency.

head.ce:title: Cost-effective route to nanodiamonds from low-rank coal and their fluorescent & dielectric characteristics

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics & Electronics, Christ (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, 560029, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics & Electronics, Christ (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, 560029, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of nanodiamonds from abundant and inexpensive precursors has recently piqued the curiosity of researchers. It has the potential to significantly reduce the cost of nanodiamonds and open up a plethora of new applications. In this work, fluorescent nanodiamonds with smaller particle sizes with rich surface functionality are synthesized from low-grade coal lignite by employing a facile acidic oxidation and ultrasonication approach. The extracted nanodiamond particles are hydrophilic and display high excitation-dependent fluorescence in the aqueous medium. The excitation-dependent fluorescence can be ascribed to the collaboration and competition of the OH and COOH functional groups. The as-synthesized nanodiamonds also show good dielectric permittivity and ac conductivity over a wide frequency range at room temperature. The present research opens up the possibility of mass production of nanodiamonds on the industrial scale from a low-cost precursor.

ce:section.ce:para: Coal, an organic carbon-based resource, plays a vital role in meeting our society's energy requirements [1–7]. Over a significant period, it is used as a fuel in power plants and transportation, resulting in severe environmental pollution. The research community has recognized that coal's importance extends beyond its use in power production to its conversion into eco-friendly value-added materials [3,6,8–12]. The importance of coal as a precursor in the synthesis of carbon nanomaterials has been studied in recent years. It is discovered to be one of the low-cost and abundant precursors for large-scale carbon nanostructured materials production [1,5,13–17]. Other carbon-based precursors that are often employed in the synthesis of carbon nanostructures, such as hydrocarbons, graphite, graphene, and carbon nanotubes, etc., are made entirely of pure sp2 carbon. In contrast to coal, these carbon-based precursors require a lengthy extraction process and a high manufacturing cost, which precludes their commercialization.

The rank of coal has a significant effect on the properties of synthesized carbon nanomaterials [4,18–24]. Low-grade coal, in comparison to high-grade coal, is indeed very easy to extract with mild oxidizing agents and produces a high production yield [25]. Low-grade coals are gaining importance in light of the world's diminishing coal quality. Except for China, approximately 33% of coal produced worldwide is of low grade [26,27]. Studies on the utilization of low-grade coal are essential in the current scenario. The value addition of low-grade coal has become necessary.

Nanodiamonds are an important nanocarbon class with desirable characteristics such as biocompatibility, tunable surface functionalization, superior optical, mechanical, electrical, thermal properties, and stable fluorescence [28–34]. The mass production of nanodiamonds with low production cost and demanded quality is the precondition for their commercial uses [35]. This work aims to display the feasibility of synthesizing nanodiamonds from low-grade coal-lignite using a facile and inexpensive technique that could open up the possibility of mass production of nanodiamonds on an industrial scale.

Lignite coal was used as the precursor for the synthesis of nanodiamonds. Lignite is powdered and refluxed with 100 ml diluted HNO3 in a glass beaker followed by washing with deionized water and ultra-sonication (30 kHz) in an ultrasonicator atmospheric pressure for 1 h. The solution was then stirred for 1 h combined with heating (100 °C) at atmospheric pressure. The obtained mixture was reacted with sodium hydroxide to bring down the pH to neutral. Then the smaller particles were separated by centrifugation, dried, and labelled as ND.

One drop of ND solution was pipetted out and dispersed onto a carbon support film on a copper grid, and TEM studies were performed using a JEOL JEM-2100 model through a unique drop method. The TEM images were processed with Image J software. Atomic force microscopy image was taken using the AFM instrument (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction studies were performed in a powder X-ray Diffractometer (type: Rigaku) with the starting angle of 3° to a stopping angle of 79° with Cu Kα radiation (k = 1.5418 Å) at 40 kV, 100 mA. Raman spectrum was recorded on a Renishaw confocal Raman spectrometer. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum was acquired using a Thermo Nicolet 370 spectrophotometer. The XPS measurements were carried out utilising an Omicron-metal ultrahigh vacuum (UHV) system equipped with a monochromatic Al K X-ray source (h = 1486.6 eV) and a multi-channeltron hemispherical electron energy analyzer (EA 125). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurements of ND were conducted with a Netzsch DSC 204 F1. UV Visible absorption analyses were recorded using Ocean optics JAZ series instrument. Fluorescence spectrum measurements were performed using an RF-5301 PC, Shimadzu fluorescence spectrometer. The dielectric measurements were performed with an LCR meter (LCR-8101G).

The surface morphology of the synthesized ND was studied in detail with HRTEM images, and Selected Area Diffraction pattern is depicted in Figs. 1 and 2
.

The as-synthesized nanodiamonds comprise diverse crystals of monocrystalline and polycrystalline nature. They exhibit accordion twins, which are folded, pleat-like lattice planes (Fig. 1a) and twinning planes with multiple folds (Fig. 1b) [5,36,37]. The spacing of the crystal lattice fringes was measured to be in the range of 2.0–2.1 Å. This is in good agreement with the lattice planes of various diamond phases, including the cubic diamond (111) (2.06 Å), lonsdaleite (002) (2.06 Å), and lonsdaleite (100) (2.18 Å) as reported elsewhere [35–37].
Electronic diffraction pattern (Fig. 2) shows the diffraction ring of the (111), (220) and (311) diamond planes (dhkl = 0.207 nm, 0.125 nm, 0.107 nm) [35,38,39].

The ultrasonication waves create dangling bonds on the surface of nanodiamond particles, which react in later stages to form strong carbon-carbon covalent inter-particle bonds, graphitic shells (which engulfs several nanodiamond primary particles and hold them together in a strongly bonded aggregate), or surface functional groups [40]. A. Krueger et al. [41]proposed a hierarchical model for nanodiamond aggregates, classifying them as agglomerates (20–30 nm), intermediate aggregates (2–3 nm), and core aggregates (100–200 nm). The agglomerates and intermediate aggregates in this study are disintegrated by moderate or powerful sonication. The core aggregates are very robust and shall be disintegrated in the future studies using a technique of stirred-media milling with micron-sized ceramic beads proposed in the patent by A. Krueger et al. [41].

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was also employed to determine the average grain size of the as-synthesized ND. Atomic force microscope is used in tapping mode, where a laser stimulates the cantilever close to its resonant frequency, causing the tip to resonate. Given that nanodiamonds are often symmetrical in form, height measurements also validate their average size. The measured particle sizes from the AFM image (ranging from 3.67 to 5 nm) (Fig. 5 ) are also within the expected range as validated using HRTEM.

The XRD spectrum of the sample (Fig. 6 ) exhibits a peak located at ̴ 44° that can be assigned to the (111) lattice plane of a cubic diamond, which is consistent with the electron diffraction pattern results [5,37]. The sharp peak around 2θ = 27° is assigned to (002) graphite.

Raman spectroscopy is a non-destructive approach for detecting carbon crystal structures and determining their quality and composition. It works by scattering light from vibrational modes of Raman active molecules. Raman spectroscopy of NDs is useful for more than simply identifying the diamond phase; it also offers important insights into the structure, composition, and surface functionalities. The Raman spectrum of the as-synthesized ND is shown in Fig. 7 . Raman spectrum was deconvoluted into four distinct bands at 1235 cm−1, 1330 cm−1, 1428 cm−1 (D peak) and 1574 cm−1 (G peak). The peak at 1235 cm−1 is produced by the coherence scattering of the smallest ND grains [42]. The spectral characteristics of ND show a typical Raman profile of NDs, with a significant band at 1330 cm−1 ascribed to the ND lattice, indicating the production of nanodiamond-like carbon which is also evident from XRD analysis and TEM micrographs. The peaks at 1428 cm−1 (D peak) and 1574 cm−1 (G peak) are attributed to the disordered sp2 carbon present in the sample and first order scattering of the E2g vibration of graphene respectively [43–47].

FTIR spectrum of ND (Fig. 8 ) was taken to study the functional groups attached to ND. The spectrum shows a stretching vibration of OH at 3400 cm−1 and OH bending vibrations at 1600 cm−1 [48,49]. The other major peak of ND consists of carboxyl functionalization. From the literature, we can deduce that the function of carboxyl groups (–COOH and –OH groups) are identical to hydrogen termination, which in turn enhance the stability of nanodiamonds in ambient environments. From the literature, nanodiamonds are found to more stable than graphite as a result of hydrogen termination and sizes between 3 and 6 nm [5].

To further investigate the carbon and oxygen bonding environment of the as-synthesized ND, XPS analysis was conducted. The as-synthesized nanodiamond XPS spectra are fitted with a Lorentz–Gauss algorithm after a Shirley background subtraction. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of ND is demonstrated in Fig. 9 a and is deconvoluted into four peaks (Fig. 9a). The significant peak with the highest intensity at 283 eV is assigned to sp3 diamond-like carbon, whereas the small peak at 284.6 eV is attributed to the presence of sp2 graphitic carbon [42,50]. The peaks at higher binding energies are assigned to various surface oxygen functionalities of ND. The peak at a binding energy of 287.2 eV corresponds to C–O (of phenyl, hydroxyl, epoxy, and ester groups), and the peak at 289.6 eV can be attributed to C = O (of carbonyl, carboxyl, ketone, and ester groups).

As shown in Fig. 9b, the high-resolution O1s XPS spectra of ND were deconvoluted into two peaks. The peaks at binding energies of 531.4 eV and 532.8 eV correspond to C = O (of carboxyl groups) and C–OH (of hydroxyl groups). The XPS spectrum reveals that ND is enriched with different oxygen functional groups, dominated by hydroxyl group, as supported by FTIR spectral analyses. The hydrophilic nature of the as-synthesized nanodiamonds is demonstrated in the Fig. 10.

Coal comprises of macromolecular structures, contrary to graphite's lattice structure. As a result of the substantial differences in their textures, the phase transformation of coal-derived carbon varies considerably from that of graphite. Ultrasonic energy has the potential to be a useful technique in the production of nanodiamonds [51,52]. Furthermore, it is a very efficient tool for ultrasonic dispersion, disaggregating, and functionalizing the produced nanodiamonds. The strategy whereby the nanodiamonds were produced was hypothesized to be the same as that reported elsewhere [35]. However, in this scenario, ultrasound energy is employed, resulting in very strong effects and ultrasonic cavitation locally. Extremely high temperatures (about 5000 K) and pressures (about 2000 atm) are attained locally during the development of ultrasonic cavitation, which may be the point of origin for the synthesis of nano diamonds [51,52]. Low-grade Indian coals have been found to feature graphite-like polyaromatic structures, with the irregular and polyaromatic hydrocarbons in the coal being connected by very weak linkages and dissociated during ultrasonication. The polymerized aromatic fragments were then further divided into C2 carbon units, which may ultimately result in the creation of nanodiamonds through polymorphic reaction [5,53,54]. The production of nanodiamonds was also hypothesized based on Wang and Yang's nanothermodynamic theory of metastable phase nucleation and growth materials at the nanometer size [55]. The hydrogen found in precursor coals also serves a major role in the development of nanodiamonds, as reported elsewhere [5,37,56]. For the nanodiamonds to attain stability, the surfaces of the sp3 carbon must either be stabilized with functional groups or reformed into sp2 carbon [57]. In this study, from HRTEM, it is observed that there is no distinct graphitic shell that surrounds the ND. As a result, we rule out the possibility of a sp3 to sp2 surface transformation. In this study, several hydroxyl and oxygen-containing functional groups stabilize the ND. The thermodynamic stability of surface functionalization by hydroxyl and oxygen-containing functional groups on NDs has been simulated by Barnard et al. [58]. Barnard and the team reported that the oxygen and hydroxyl terminations on nanodiamonds are thermodynamically favourable and form strong C–O covalent bonds. The C–O bond is clearly demonstrated in our Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. 8). The roles of oxidized functional groups (–COOH and –OH groups), which stabilize these nanodiamonds, appear to be identical to hydrogen termination [5].

Krueger et al. examined the latest developments in the surface functionalization of NDs, and they mentioned that most organic functional groups, including hydrogen and oxidized group termination, can be formed on the diamond surface, demonstrating that NDs may be stabilized by a variety of groups with distinct characteristics and are not confined to a specific one [59].

DSC thermogram was taken to study the heat flux versus temperature or time (Fig. 11). It mainly shows two peaks representing a) endothermic and b) exothermic reactions indicating water evaporation and oxygen reactions of the ND, respectively [60]. The endothermic peak indicates water evaporation, and the exothermic peak reflects the oxygen reactions of the ND [60]. Since the analysis was performed in the presence of air, the starting point of the exothermic peak can be considered as the ignition temperature, and the peak provides information about the combustion features of ND [61]. The net effect of DSC analysis is exothermic with the temperature of the peak maximum in the range of 273.31 °C. An enthalpy value of 0.2 kJg−1 was determined from the enthalpy measurements. The ignition temperature (T i), the temperature of peak maximum (T m), and the enthalpy value (ϵ) are observed to be 229.78 °C, 273.31 °C, and 0.2 kJ g−1 respectively.
To explore the optical properties of ND, UV visible absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum are studied.

The UV visible absorption spectrum of ND in water (Fig. 12 ) exhibits an intense absorption band centered at the deep UV region (233 nm), which can be attributed to the intrinsic absorption of nanodiamonds (σ-σ̽ transition), which matches with the energy gap of diamond (5.5 eV). There is a shoulder peak around 276 nm which is assigned to the n-π* transition of the CO radical [35,62].

To investigate the fluorescence, emission spectrum of ND upon varying the excitation wavelength from 274 nm to 500 nm in intervals of 20 nm were observed (Fig. 13 a). The fluorescence emission spectrum of ND show excitation-dependent: a) blue emission and b) green emission. Blue fluorescence was observed when the excitation wavelength was varied from 274 nm to 374 nm, followed by green fluorescence for excitation wavelengths 394 nm–474 nm. Each functional group in ND has optimal excitation and emission bands. In particular, the OH groups are prominent at first, and the greatest emission is blue when excited by UV light. Simultaneously, this excitation wavelength can also produce green and yellow emission, but their fluorescence intensity is lesser than that of blue emission. Then when excited by the blue light, the CO functional groups dominate, resulting in the strongest emission shift to green color. The fluorescence origins of excitation-dependent nanodiamonds influenced by OH and CO functional groups are illustrated in Figs. 13b and 14 . Therefore, the fluorescence of ND is attributed to the cooperation and competition between OH and COOH functional groups (Fig. 14) [35].

The cooperation is reflected in the variation of the color of fluorescence from blue to green when excited at various energies. The competition is expressed in the shift of the optimum excitation and emission wavelengths, that is, the position of the strongest emission peak [63,64]. Due to the fact that each functional group has optimum excitation and emission wavelengths, when the excitation wavelength is changed, the corresponding emission wavelength varies correspondingly, resulting in an excitation-dependent fluorescence. FTIR spectrum analysis also confirms the dominance of hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups in ND. The fluorescence spectrum ranges from 380 nm to 700 nm, with an fwhm of ̴110–170 nm, which concludes that it can excite multiple bands at the same time [65].

In order to study the stability of the fluorescence emissions, the variation of fluorescence intensity, fluorescence peak maxima, and stokes shift with the change in excitation wavelength were extracted from the fluorescence spectrum, as shown in Fig. 15 .

The Stokes shift is the difference (in energy, wavenumber, or frequency units) between the position of the emission maxima and the excitation spectrum. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the stokes shift exhibits a linear behaviour (Fig. 15a). Negative slopes with excellent linear behaviour suggest the high stability of excited states. The large value of stokes shift found for the initial excitation wavelengths is an advantage for bio-imaging applications. This stability can also be confirmed by the relatively steady fluorescence emission peaks under different excitations (Fig. 15b). The fluorescence emission spectrum shows a hyperchromic shift till an excitation of 454 nm (Fig. 15c).

Fluorescence spectrum were taken in different pH mediums (acidic, neutral, and basic), varying the excitation wavelength from 274 nm to 454 nm (Fig. 16 ). The emission peak position is pH value independent, and the emission maxima peak intensity varies slightly with different pH. There is no photobleaching even after a UV exposure of 24 h.

To explore the electrical characteristics of ND, dielectric constant, conductivity, and impedance were measured. Fig. 17 a, b shows the real and imaginary part of the dielectric variation with frequencies analyzed in the range of 20 Hz to 1 MHz. A high-value dielectric constant at low frequencies is because, under the influence of the applied electric field, the charge carriers existing in the ND migrate some distance. These charge carriers are blocked by electrodes resulting in a space charge region. This leads to an increase of tan δ at high frequency. As the frequency increases, the polarization decreases and reaches a constant value where the electronic and atomic contribution is prominent, and the dielectric constant is independent of frequency [8]. The imaginary part of the dielectric strength also shows a similar trend to the real part. The sample's microstructure and porosity increase dipolar and space charge polarization due to interfacial polarization at the boundary and accumulation of electric charge at the interfaces at low frequency [8,66]. The independence of the dielectric constant at high frequency indicates that the ND is an ideal candidate for high-frequency electronic devices with small dielectric loss. The real part of the complex AC conductivity is shown in Fig. 17c. The enhancement of ac conductivity may also be explained based on excess ion vacancies due to the high conduction of the space charge layer. The frequency-dependent conductivity indicates that this enhancement is due to grain size rather than grain boundary or surface conduction. The ND can be used as a frequency-dependent capacitor at low frequency.

Fig. 18
depicts the response of the complex impedance with frequency. The decrease in real (Z′) impedance with an increase in frequency is attributed to the space charge polarization. This space charge polarization is reduced to a meagre value at high frequency.

To sum up, we demonstrated a facile approach for the production of nanodiamonds from low-quality coal feedstock. Most of the conventional synthesis procedures need rigorous conditions and are inadequate for large-scale manufacturing, which is disappointing since nanodiamonds offer intriguing properties that might find various uses in the biomedical field, like drug delivery, biosensing, coating for implantable materials, and electronic devices. The procedure described in this study would provide a convenient alternative method for the low-cost mass production of nanodiamonds. The as-synthesized nanodiamonds are highly stable and exhibit blue and green fluorescence emission in the aqueous medium. The independence of the dielectric constant of the as-synthesized ND at high frequency implies that these nanoparticles are suitable candidates for higher frequency range electronic devices with small dielectric loss. The use of this low-cost precursor and facile method will lead to a considerable fall in nanodiamonds' price and an expansion of the applications. Furthermore, our future research would be subjected to a comprehensive investigation of the structural characteristics and distinctive attributes that opens up diverse applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Center for Research, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, for providing necessary facilities for the successful completion of this work. We also acknowledge the CeNSE, Bangalore; M G University, Kottayam; and SAIF, CUSAT, Cochin, for the samples' characterization.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coal derived carbon nanomaterials – recent advances in synthesis and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.C. Hoang, M. Hassan, V.G. Gomes, Coal derived carbon nanomaterials - Recent advances in synthesis and applications, Appl. Mater. Today. 12 (2018) 342-358. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apmt.2018.06.007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene sheets from graphitized anthracite coal: preparation, decoration, and application

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on catalytic-pyrolysis of coal and biomass for value-added fuel and chemicals, Energy Sources, Part A Recover

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of preformed mixed nano-carbon structure from low rank coal

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanodiamonds from coal under ambient conditions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xiao, P. Liu, G.W. Yang, Nanodiamonds from coal under ambient conditions, Nanoscale. 7 (2015) 6114-6125. https://doi.org/10.1039/c4nr06186a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemicals and materials from coal in the 21st century

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.H. Schobert, C. Song, Chemicals and materials from coal in the 21st century, Fuel. 81 (2002) 15-32. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0016-2361(00)00203-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable direct band gap photoluminescent organic semiconducting nanoparticles from lignite

Turgut, Effects of ultrasonication and conventional mechanical homogenization processes on the structures and dielectric properties of BaTiO3 ceramics, Ultrason. Sonochem. 34 (2017) 873-880. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2016.07.027.

Jia, Coal-Derived Graphene Quantum Dots Produced by Ultrasonic Physical Tailoring and Their Capacity for Cu(II) Detection, ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng. 7 (2019) 9793-9799. https://doi.org/10.1021/acssuschemeng.8b06792.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biologically derived value-added products from coal

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.K. Polman, C.R. Breckenridge, D.L. Stoner, G.F. Andrews, Biologically derived value-added products from coal, Appl. Biochem. Biotechnol. 54 (1995) 249-255. https://doi.org/10.1007/BF02787923.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Formation of carbon nano-balls and carbon nano-tubes from northeast Indian Tertiary coal: value added products from low grade coal

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Das, B.K. Saikia, B.P. Baruah, Formation of carbon nano-balls and carbon nano-tubes from northeast Indian Tertiary coal: Value added products from low grade coal, Gondwana Res. 31 (2016) 295-304. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gr.2015.01.012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microbial degradation of coal into a value added product
